title: Acts 10
subtitle: Peter and Cornelius — The Gentile Pentecost

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Peter and Cornelius

sections.header: Verses 1—23 — Cornelius’ Vision; Peter’s Trance: Do Not Call Anything Impure

heb.paragraph: used both of Cornelius (vv.2, 7) and of his devout soldier. Describes Gentiles who revered the God of Israel, attended synagogue, gave to the poor, and prayed — without formal conversion to Judaism. The 'God-fearer' category was documented in first-century synagogue inscriptions (notably the Aphrodisias inscription, discovered 1976).

Peter's trance (ekstasis — a going-out of oneself) is distinct from Cornelius's vision (horama). Together they show divine orchestration converging from two directions on a single moment — neither man initiates; both respond to heaven's direction.

places._raw_html:
Places

Caesarea Maritima
32.5000° N, 34.9000° E
The Roman administrative capital of Judea, built by Herod the Great as a showcase Greco-Roman port city named for Caesar Augustus. Home of the Roman prefect and military garrison. Cornelius's presence as a centurion is historically plausible — Caesarea was the Roman military headquarters for the region.
Rooftop at Joppa
32.0543° N, 34.7510° E
Peter's trance occurs on a housetop (dōma — flat roof). Mediterranean houses commonly had flat rooftops used for prayer and rest. Peter's elevation — above street level — mirrors the vision's upward movement: the sheet comes down from heaven while Peter is raised above the ground.

tl.name: Cornelius — The Gentile Pentecost
tl.text: Peter is led by vision to the household of Cornelius in Caesarea. The Holy Spirit falls on the Gentiles before baptism — the 'Gentile Pentecost.' The Gentile mission is divinely inaugurated, not humanly planned.

panels.ctx: The 'God-fearers' (theosebeis, phoboumenoi ton theon) were Gentiles who had adopted Jewish monotheism and ethics without completing formal conversion. They attended synagogues, observed some Jewish practices, and gave to Jewish causes. They were a significant population across the Diaspora world and became a primary harvest field for Paul's Gentile mission. Peter's sheet vision (vv.11–16) is commonly read as abolishing food laws — but its primary application is stated explicitly in v.28: 'God has shown me that I should not call anyone impure or unclean.' The food imagery is the vehicle; the human application is the point.

cross.note: "He declared all foods clean" — Jesus's teaching that Luke applies practically in Acts 10. The abolition of food distinctions was theologically prepared for; Acts 10 is its social-missional application.
"The foreigners who bind themselves to the Lord... these I will bring to my holy mountain." The OT promise of Gentile inclusion that Cornelius's reception fulfils.
"To the ends of the earth" — Cornelius represents the first formal fulfilment of the Gentile stage of the commission.

notes.note: MacArthur on the sheet vision: the command "Kill and eat" applied to unclean animals is not primarily about dietary law but about the theological principle of divine declaration overriding human categories. God has declared the Gentiles "clean" — not because they are morally superior but because his redemptive work has been applied to them.

"Do not call anything impure that God has made clean" — MacArthur treats this as the charter verse for Gentile inclusion. The passive "has made clean" is divine action: God is the subject. The Gentile mission is not a human initiative but the execution of a divine verdict.

Calvin on Cornelius's prayers and alms 'coming up as a memorial offering': God takes note of sincere seeking, even when incomplete, and responds by providing the full Gospel message. Cornelius's piety was evidence of a seeking heart that God would complete — not a meriting of salvation but a preparedness to receive it.

Calvin on 'Do not call anything impure that God has made clean': the categories of clean and unclean that structured OT covenant life have been fulfilled and transcended in Christ. Their removal is the sign that the reality they pointed to has arrived.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "God-fearing" (phoboumenos ton theon) was a recognized social category documented in synagogue inscriptions (the Aphrodisias inscription lists God-fearers alongside Jews). Cornelius is representative of a known population of Gentile seekers attached to Judaism.

The "sheet" let down by its four corners suggests symbolic universality — the four corners of the earth. The NET notes this reading is supported by the vision's application to people from every nation (v.35).

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes the precise categories for Cornelius: devout (eusebēs), God-fearing, generous to the poor, regular in prayer. These four qualities describe the ideal God-fearer — a Gentile who had come as close to the covenant community as Judaism's boundary markers permitted. The Gospel now comes to remove those barriers.

"Surely not, Lord!" — Robertson notes this is the fourth time Peter says "surely not, Lord" in the Gospels and Acts (cf. Matt 16:22; John 13:8). Peter's consistent pattern of well-intentioned resistance to divine direction makes the vision's three-fold repetition pedagogically necessary.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Keener provides extensive background on Roman centurions in the NT. Centurions commanded approximately eighty soldiers and were the backbone of the Roman military — typically career soldiers of proven reliability. The NT consistently portrays centurions favourably (Luke 7:1–10; 23:47; Acts 27:43).

Keener notes that Cornelius's prayers and alms "have come up as a memorial offering before God" echoes the incense offering rising before the LORD in the Temple. Cornelius's non-Temple piety has been received by God as an offering — a remarkable statement about the reach of divine grace before formal Gospel proclamation.

sections.header: Verses 24—48 — Peter Preaches to Cornelius; The Spirit Falls on the Gentiles

heb.paragraph: literally 'does not receive a face.' The Hebrew idiom nasa panim (lift the face) meant to show partiality based on status. Peter's declaration in v.34 is a direct reversal of everything Jewish ethnic-religious boundary theology had maintained.

this absolute title for Christ appears only here in Acts. Not Lord of Israel, not Lord of believers, but Lord of all peoples — the theological foundation for the universal Gentile mission. If Christ is Lord of all, the Gospel must reach all.

places._raw_html:
Places

Cornelius's House — Caesarea
32.5000° N, 34.9000° E
A substantial dwelling capable of hosting 'relatives and close friends' (v.24). Luke's precision about the gathering — family, friends, possibly servants — suggests a significant household of social standing. The Gospel's first formal Gentile reception is not a private meeting but a community event.
Caesarea — Roman Capital
32.5000° N, 34.9000° E
Caesarea Maritima was the seat of Roman power in Judea — home to the prefect and the military headquarters. That the Gentile Pentecost occurs here (in the Roman administrative centre) rather than in the Jewish capital carries programmatic significance for the mission's direction toward Rome.

tl.name: Gentile Pentecost — Caesarea
tl.text: The Holy Spirit falls on Cornelius's household before they are baptized, while Peter is still preaching. This 'Gentile Pentecost' establishes the pattern: God's acceptance precedes and grounds the church's formal reception.

panels.ctx: Peter's address to Cornelius's household (vv.34–43) is the most compact and complete summary of the Gospel kerygma in Acts — moving from John's baptism through Jesus's ministry, death, resurrection, and appearances to the commission to witness, ending with the offer of forgiveness. It is the template for apostolic preaching. The Spirit falls while Peter is still speaking — before baptism — demonstrating that the order is God's to determine. Peter's conclusion (v.47: 'Can anyone keep these people from being baptized? They have received the Holy Spirit just as we have') shows that baptism follows the Spirit's work as its public seal, not its prerequisite.

cross.note: The Pentecost pattern: repent → be baptized → receive the Spirit. The Cornelius episode inverts the Spirit-baptism order, demonstrating that the sequence is flexible while the substance (faith, repentance, Spirit, baptism) remains constant.
Quoted at Pentecost: "I will pour out my Spirit on all people" — the "all" is now visibly fulfilled as the Spirit falls on Gentiles.

Peter's later testimony at the Jerusalem Council recalls the Cornelius episode as decisive evidence: 'God, who knows the heart, showed that he accepted them by giving the Holy Spirit to them, just as he did to us.'

notes.note: MacArthur carefully distinguishes Peter's statement about God accepting those who fear him from universalism. Verse 36 immediately clarifies: the "message God sent" is the Gospel of peace through Jesus Christ. Cornelius's piety had not saved him — it had prepared him to receive the Gospel.

MacArthur on "Can anyone keep these people from being baptized?" — Peter uses the baptism of the Spirit as the argument for water baptism: if God has already accepted them, no human can withhold the public seal. Where God has given the Spirit, the church must not withhold baptism on cultural or ethnic grounds.

Calvin on 'God does not show favoritism' — the demolition of all national, ethnic, and religious privilege before God. The Jew has no advantage over the Greek in the matter of divine acceptance. All stand equally before the grace that accepts those who fear God in every nation.

Calvin on the Spirit falling before baptism: God's deliberate reversal of the expected order establishes that baptism is a sign of grace already given, not a means of conferring it. The sacrament seals what the Spirit has already done.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "Lord of all" (pantōn Kyrios) is an unusual title for Christ in Acts, found only here. Its absolute scope is the theological foundation for the universal Gentile mission: if Christ is Lord of all peoples, the Gospel must reach all peoples.

The NET notes that Peter "ordered" (prosetaxen) baptism — a command, not a suggestion. The community is the agent; Peter directs the community's action. This is baptism as ecclesial act — the church receiving into its number those the Spirit has already received into God's family.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on Peter's summary: 'accepts from every nation the one who fears him and does what is right' is not universalism (salvation apart from Christ) but the summary of what genuine seeking looks like in every culture. The Cornelius episode shows God responding to genuine seeking by providing the full Gospel.

Robertson on the Spirit falling "while Peter was still speaking" — the imperfect (elalountos) indicates Peter had not finished. Luke's timing is precise: God does not wait for the invitation or the altar call. The Spirit acts in sovereign freedom during the proclamation of the word.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The phrase "Jesus Christ, who is Lord of all" (Kyrios pantōn) is a direct challenge to the Roman imperial title Dominus et Deus noster claimed by Domitian and echoed in earlier imperial ideology. Luke's proclamation of Christ as "Lord of all" is politically charged in the Caesarean military headquarters context.

Keener notes that tongues at Cornelius's house function as the sign that the Gentile believers have received the same Spirit as the Jewish believers at Pentecost. Without a visible, audible sign, Peter and the circumcised believers would have had no external evidence. The tongues serve a specific ecclesiological function.

ppl.name: Paul↗ People
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Peter↗ People
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon bar Jonah) dominates Acts 1–12 as the church’s primary spokesman and miracle-worker. He preaches at Pentecost (Acts 2), heals at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3), defends before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4–5), raises Tabitha (Acts 9:36–42), and receives the vision that opens the gospel to Gentiles (Acts 10). His release from Herod’s prison by an angel (Acts 12) closes his narrative in Acts; Paul’s story takes over from ch.13.

ppl.name: Cornelius↗ People
ppl.role: Roman centurion; first Gentile convert; pivot of Acts 10
ppl.text: Cornelius is a God-fearing Roman centurion at Caesarea — devout, generous, and prayerful but uncircumcised. His angel-directed encounter with Peter (Acts 10) is the theological turning point of Acts: the Spirit falls on Gentiles before baptism, demonstrating that God shows no partiality. Peter’s report to Jerusalem (Acts 11) establishes the principle that will be formalized at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVCornelius stared at him in fear. \ESVAnd he stared at him in terror and said, “What is it, Lord?” And he said to him, “Your prayers and your alms have ascended as a memorial before God.NIVAbout noon the following day as they were on their journey and approaching the city, Peter went up on the roof to pray.ESVThe next day, as they were on their journey and approaching the city, Peter went up on the housetop about the sixth hour to pray.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.
